input.prompt:
After upgrading to MSTest 4.1.0, my test project fails to compile. I have a sealed
custom TestMethodAttribute subclass that overrides Execute, and several tests use
[Timeout(TestTimeout.Infinite)]. How do I fix these errors?

data.content:

[Timeout(TestTimeout.Infinite)]. How do I fix these errors?

data.reasoningText:
Two issues in MSTest 4.1.0:

1. **`TestMethodAttribute.Execute(ITestMethod)` is no longer virtual/overridable.** The class needs to be unsealed and use the new extensibility model. Actually, looking at this more carefully - in MSTest v3, `TestMethodAttribute` had a virtual `Execute` method. In MSTest 4.x, this was removed/changed. The new approach is to not subclass `TestMethodAttribute` but instead implement `ITestMethodDecorator` or similar.

Wait, let me think about what actually changed in MSTest 4.1.0. The `Execute` method on `TestMethodAttribute` was removed as a virtual method. 

The recommended approach now is to implement the `ITestMethodDecorator` interface instead. Actually, I need to check what the actual MSTest 4.x migration path is. In MSTest v3, `TestMethodAttribute` had a virtual `Execute` method that returned `TestResult[]`. In MSTest 4.x, they changed the extensibility model entirely - the `Execute` method is no longer virtual, so the old override pattern doesn't work anymore.

I'm also noticing that `TestTimeout.Infinite` was removed, so I'd need to use `Timeout.Infinite` from `System.Threading` instead. Let me check what's actually available in the MSTest 4.1.0 package to see what extensibility options are there.

data.reasoningText:
So in MSTest 4.1.0:
1. `Execute` was replaced by `ExecuteAsync` - the method signature changed from `TestResult[] Execute(ITestMethod)` to `Task<TestResult[]> ExecuteAsync(ITestMethod)` (or similar async pattern)
2. `TestTimeout` was removed

Let me check the exact signature and also what replaced `TestTimeout.Infinite`.

arguments.description: Check ExecuteAsync signature and Timeout constants

toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-1ee6fe2826ed42a099b3a5498e7c734b && grep -B2 -A10 'ExecuteAsync' ~/.nuget/packages/mstest.testframework/4.1.0/lib/net8.0/MSTest.TestFramework.xml && echo \"===\" && grep -i 'timeout\\|infinite' ~/.nuget/packages/mstest.testframework/4.1.0/lib/net8.0/MSTest.TestFramework.xml | head -20", "description": "Check ExecuteAsync signature and Timeout constants", "initial_wait": 10}

            <param name="retryContext">An object to encapsulate the state needed for retry execution.</param>

            the last added element is used to determine the test outcome.
            The other results are currently not used, but may be used in the future for tooling to show the

            <summary>
            Retries the test method <see cref="P:Microsoft.VisualStudio.TestTools.UnitTesting.RetryAttribute.MaxRetryAttempts"/> times in case of failure.
            Note that a first run of the method was already executed and failed before this method is called.
            </summary>
            <param name="retryContext">An object to encapsulate the state needed for retry execution.</param>
            <returns>
            Returns a <see cref="T:Microsoft.VisualStudio.TestTools.UnitTesting.RetryResult"/> object that contains the results of all attempts. Only
            the last added element is used to determine the test outcome.
            The other results are currently not used, but may be used in the future for tooling to show the
            state of the failed attempts.
--
            </summary>

data.content:
Build succeeds with 0 errors. Here's what changed in MSTest 4.1.0 and how I fixed it:

1. **`Execute` → `ExecuteAsync`**: The synchronous `TestResult[] Execute(ITestMethod)` override was replaced by `Task<TestResult[]> ExecuteAsync(ITestMethod)`. Updated the override to be `async` and `await` the base call.

2. **`TestTimeout.Infinite` → `Timeout.Infinite`**: The `TestTimeout` enum was removed. Replaced with `System.Threading.Timeout.Infinite` (value `-1`), which the `[Timeout]` attribute still accepts.

The remaining warnings (MSTEST0032, MSTEST0057) are pre-existing analyzer suggestions, not errors.
